CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Preliminaries → Method → Complexity → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contribution early (an algorithm to perform polynomial expansions on CSR without densification). However, presentation suffers from notation and formatting inconsistencies (e.g. printed complexities like “O(dkDk)” that appear to mean O(d^k D^k), triangular-number formulas such as “T2(n) = 12n(n + 1)” and mixed 0/1-based indexing), a few typographical errors (“efter”), an unresolved placeholder in the pseudocode (line 24 ckn = ?), and references to a plot/figures that are not included. These issues make some derivations and the empirical discussion harder to follow and would benefit from careful proofreading and clearer, consistent math typesetting.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper describes a practical algorithm to compute k-degree polynomial feature expansions directly on CSR matrices (Sections 4–5) by using explicit combinatorial index mappings (triangular/tetrahedral numbers) to place sparse products without densification and shows a complexity of O(d^k D^k) (Section 6). This is a useful and clearly-articulated engineering contribution, but the core idea—using combinatorial numbering to index k-combinations and exploiting sparsity in CSR—is a straightforward application of known combinatorial/indexing techniques rather than a fundamentally new theoretical concept. Thus the work is moderately novel and incremental: notable for practical utility and clear differentiation from dense methods, but not a highly original methodological breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The core technical idea — enumerate only k-combinations from the dD nonzeros and map those k-tuples into flat Ak columns via nested triangular/tetrahedral-number offsets (Sec. 4) — is sound, and the combinatorial complexity derivation in Sec. 6.1 (binomial(dD+k-1,k) ≃ O((dD)^k)) correctly yields the claimed density-dependent speedup. However the paper contains indexing/notation typos in the mapping formulas (Eqs. 3, 6–7 and some 1/2 factors) and does not give a concise, formal bijectivity proof for the general-k mapping. The experimental methodology (Sec. 6.2) is weak: only one baseline (scikit-learn), no statistical tests beyond mean±3σ, and — importantly — the time to densify the input for the standard method was explicitly not counted, undermining the fairness of the comparison. Overall the technical claims are plausible and mostly supported, but there are nontrivial gaps in formal rigor and in the empirical evaluation.
